hero-title: Why this is worth your time
lead: Lots of work already uses AI a little, and that's a good thing. The goal isn't to do more and more. The goal is to let AI take the dull jobs off your hands, so you can keep the part of the work that really needs a person.
AI is already part of how a lot of work gets done, and that's good news. The point isn't to do more. It's to hand off the boring bits and keep the part that needs a person.
AI is already part of how a lot of work gets done — and that's good news. The win isn't doing more; it's getting the boring bits off your plate and keeping the part that needs a person.
AI already does much of the work, and that helps. The win isn't more output — it's shedding the busywork and keeping what needs a person.
AI already does much of the work. The win: shed busywork, keep what needs a person.
enduring-idea: The one idea to keep: you don't have to race against AI. You just give it the dull, repeating jobs, and you keep the thinking and the deciding. Working well with AI is something anyone can learn, and even a little practice helps a lot.
The one idea to keep: you don't compete with AI. You hand it the busywork and keep the judgment. Working with it is a skill you can learn, and a little goes a long way.
The one idea to keep: you don't compete with AI — you hand it the busywork and keep the judgment. Working with it is a learnable skill, and a little goes a long way.
The one idea to keep: don't compete with AI — delegate the busywork, keep the judgment. It's a learnable skill, and a little goes far.
Keep this: don't compete with AI — delegate busywork, keep judgment. Learnable; a little goes far.

week-hint: Give the busywork blocks to AI — tap one, or drag across a few. The outlined blocks are the part that needs you, and they can never be given away.

the-reason: That is the whole reason it's worth your time. AI takes on the jobs that repeat. You keep the things only a person can do: reading the mood in a room, weighing what is fair, holding the background story that never shows up in the numbers, and knowing when the "right" answer is actually the wrong one. The edge isn't having AI — everyone has that now. The edge is being good at telling it what to do. And that is exactly what you can learn here.

That's the whole reason it's worth your time. AI takes the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can do — reading a room, weighing what's fair, carrying the context that never makes it into the numbers, and knowing when the "right" answer is the wrong one. The edge isn't access to AI; everyone has that. It's the skill of directing it — and that's exactly what you can learn here.

That's the whole reason it's worth your time. AI takes the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can do — reading a room, weighing what's fair, carrying the context that never makes it into a dataset, and deciding when the "correct" answer is the wrong one. The edge isn't access to AI (everyone has that). It's the skill of directing it — and that's exactly what you can learn here.

That's the whole reason it's worth your time. AI carries the repetitive load; you keep what only a person can — reading a room, weighing fairness, holding context no dataset captures, and judging when the "correct" answer is wrong. The edge isn't access (everyone has that); it's directing it — exactly what you'll learn here.

The whole reason: AI carries repetition; you keep the human-only work — reading a room, weighing fairness, holding off-dataset context, overriding the "correct" answer. Edge isn't access (universal); it's direction — learnable here.

recall-a: Give it the boring jobs that keep repeating. Keep the people, the choices, and the last word for yourself.

tryit-intro: Think of one job you did this week that felt like a machine could do it — like writing a first draft, tidying up formatting, shortening something, or looking things up.
Pick one job from this week that felt mechanical — drafting, formatting, summarising, or looking things up.

tryit-payoff: Do that just once, and "why it's worth it" stops being something you're told. It becomes something you can plainly see for yourself.
Do that once and "why it's worth it" stops being a claim — it becomes obvious.
